tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You manage my email inbox.
  
  # Summary formatting:
  - Format the summary as a markdown document without extra comments like "Here is the summary of the mailbox:".
  - Use markdown formatting for headings and bullet points.
  - Format the summary as a list of key points and takeaways.
  
  # References
  - Use references to objects in the form of:
  @dxn:queue:data:B6INSIBY3CBEF4M5VZRYBCMAHQMPYK5AJ:01K24XMVHSZHS97SG1VTVQDM5Z:01K24XPK464FSCKVQJAB2H662M
  - References are rendered as rich content in the response to the user.
  
  # Tasks
  At the end of the summary include tasks.
  Extract only the tasks that are:
  - Directly actionable.
  - Clearly assigned to a person or team (or can easily be inferred).
  - Strongly implied by the conversation and/or user note (no speculative tasks).
  - Specific enough that someone reading them would know exactly what to do next.
  
  Format all tasks as markdown checkboxes using the syntax:
  - [ ] Task description.
  
  Additional information can be included (indented).
  
  # Email extraction
  Parse confirmation emails (e.g., flight bookings or hotel reservations) into structured objects.
  
  Note: Sending emails is handled by the "Inbox (Send)" blueprint.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.
<input>{}</input>

1. Create a Mailbox object with typename `org.dxos.type.mailbox`
2. Enable the inbox blueprint
3. Call ReadEmail operation for that mailbox

Let me start by creating the mailbox and enabling the inbox blueprint in parallel.
I'll start by creating the Mailbox object and enabling the inbox blueprint in parallel. Let me first query blueprints and create the mailbox.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You manage my email inbox.
  
  # Summary formatting:
  - Format the summary as a markdown document without extra comments like "Here is the summary of the mailbox:".
  - Use markdown formatting for headings and bullet points.
  - Format the summary as a list of key points and takeaways.
  
  # References
  - Use references to objects in the form of:
  @dxn:queue:data:B6INSIBY3CBEF4M5VZRYBCMAHQMPYK5AJ:01K24XMVHSZHS97SG1VTVQDM5Z:01K24XPK464FSCKVQJAB2H662M
  - References are rendered as rich content in the response to the user.
  
  # Tasks
  At the end of the summary include tasks.
  Extract only the tasks that are:
  - Directly actionable.
  - Clearly assigned to a person or team (or can easily be inferred).
  - Strongly implied by the conversation and/or user note (no speculative tasks).
  - Specific enough that someone reading them would know exactly what to do next.
  
  Format all tasks as markdown checkboxes using the syntax:
  - [ ] Task description.
  
  Additional information can be included (indented).
  
  # Email extraction
  Parse confirmation emails (e.g., flight bookings or hotel reservations) into structured objects.
  
  Note: Sending emails is handled by the "Inbox (Send)" blueprint.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.read-email) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.
<input>{}</input>

Let me break down the tasks:

1. Create a Mailbox object with typename `org.dxos.type.mailbox`
2. Enable the inbox blueprint
3. Call ReadEmail operation for that mailbox

Let me start by creating the mailbox and enabling the inbox blueprint in parallel.
I'll start by creating the Mailbox object and enabling the inbox blueprint in parallel. Let me first query blueprints and create the mailbox.

The inbox blueprint is now enabled. Now I need to create the mailbox object properly. The schema requires a `feed` field (a reference to an org.dxos.type.feed object) and a `filters` array. Let me first create a feed object, then create the mailbox referencing it.

Wait, I need to check if org.dxos.type.feed is a schema I can create. Let me look at the schemas list... I don't see it in the list. But the mailbox schema references it. Let me try creating a feed object anyway, or check if I can use an expando type.

Actually, looking at the schema, `feed` is a reference (`$ref`) to `dxn:org.dxos.type.feed`. 

The feed type isn't showing up in my available schemas, but it might be a built-in system type. I'll attempt to create a feed object first and see if that works, then reference it in the mailbox.

The inbox blueprint is enabled. Now I need to create the mailbox properly. The schema requires a `feed` reference (to an `org.dxos.type.feed` object) and a `filters` array. Let me first create a feed object.

The mailbox was created successfully. Now let me call read-email on this mailbox.

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You manage my email inbox.
  
  # Summary formatting:
  - Format the summary as a markdown document without extra comments like "Here is the summary of the mailbox:".
  - Use markdown formatting for headings and bullet points.
  - Format the summary as a list of key points and takeaways.
  
  # References
  - Use references to objects in the form of:
  @dxn:queue:data:B6INSIBY3CBEF4M5VZRYBCMAHQMPYK5AJ:01K24XMVHSZHS97SG1VTVQDM5Z:01K24XPK464FSCKVQJAB2H662M
  - References are rendered as rich content in the response to the user.
  
  # Tasks
  At the end of the summary include tasks.
  Extract only the tasks that are:
  - Directly actionable.
  - Clearly assigned to a person or team (or can easily be inferred).
  - Strongly implied by the conversation and/or user note (no speculative tasks).
  - Specific enough that someone reading them would know exactly what to do next.
  
  Format all tasks as markdown checkboxes using the syntax:
  - [ ] Task description.
  
  Additional information can be included (indented).
  
  # Email extraction
  Parse confirmation emails (e.g., flight bookings or hotel reservations) into structured objects.
  
  Note: Sending emails is handled by the "Inbox (Send)" blueprint.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.readEmail) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">

    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You manage my email inbox.
  
  # Summary formatting:
  - Format the summary as a markdown document without extra comments like "Here is the summary of the mailbox:".
  - Use markdown formatting for headings and bullet points.
  - Format the summary as a list of key points and takeaways.
  
  # References
  - Use references to objects in the form of:
  @dxn:queue:data:B6INSIBY3CBEF4M5VZRYBCMAHQMPYK5AJ:01K24XMVHSZHS97SG1VTVQDM5Z:01K24XPK464FSCKVQJAB2H662M
  - References are rendered as rich content in the response to the user.
  
  # Tasks
  At the end of the summary include tasks.
  Extract only the tasks that are:
  - Directly actionable.
  - Clearly assigned to a person or team (or can easily be inferred).
  - Strongly implied by the conversation and/or user note (no speculative tasks).
  - Specific enough that someone reading them would know exactly what to do next.
  
  Format all tasks as markdown checkboxes using the syntax:
  - [ ] Task description.
  
  Additional information can be included (indented).
  
  # Email extraction
  Parse confirmation emails (e.g., flight bookings or hotel reservations) into structured objects.
  
  Note: Sending emails is handled by the "Inbox (Send)" blueprint.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.readEmail) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.
<input>{}</input>

content.text:
Let me break down the task:

1. Create a Mailbox object with typename `org.dxos.type.mailbox`
2. Enable the inbox blueprint
3. Call ReadEmail operation for that mailbox

Let me start by creating the mailbox and enabling the inbox blueprint in parallel.
I'll start by creating the Mailbox object and enabling the inbox blueprint in parallel.

content.name: create-object

content.text:
The inbox blueprint was successfully enabled. However, the Mailbox creation failed because the `feed` field is required. I need to check the schema for the Mailbox type to understand what fields are needed.

Let me list schemas to find the mailbox schema.
The inbox blueprint is enabled. The mailbox creation failed because a `feed` field is required. Let me check the schema to understand the required structure.

id.description: A Universally Unique Lexicographically Sortable Identifier

jsonSchema.description: An employing organization of a person.

items.description: A Universally Unique Lexicographically Sortable Identifier

jsonSchema.description: An organization.
jsonSchema.title: Organization

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

content.text: I need to create a feed first, then reference it in the mailbox. The mailbox requires a `feed` field that references an `org.dxos.type.feed` object. Let me create the feed first.
I see — the mailbox requires a `feed` reference. I need to first create a Feed object, then create the Mailbox referencing it.
Now I have a feed object with ID 01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTD1. Let me create the mailbox with a reference to this feed.
Feed created. Now let me create the Mailbox with the feed reference.
Mailbox created successfully. Now let me call read-email on this mailbox.

All three tasks are complete:
1. Mailbox object exists in the database (id: 01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTEJ)
2. Inbox blueprint was successfully enabled
3. read-email was called and completed successfully (returned empty content as expected since there are no messages)
All three criteria have been met. Let me complete the job.

success.summary:
All completion criteria met successfully:
1. **Mailbox object created** — A mailbox named 'Test Mailbox' exists in the database (ID: 01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTEJ, type: org.dxos.type.mailbox) with a linked feed (ID: 01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTD1).
2. **Inbox blueprint enabled** — org.dxos.blueprint.inbox was successfully enabled, providing tools: readEmail, draftEmail, classifyEmail, googleMailSync, extractMessage.
3. **read-email called successfully** — The read-email operation completed without error, returning empty content (as expected with no messages in the feed).

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You manage my email inbox.
  
  # Summary formatting:
  - Format the summary as a markdown document without extra comments like "Here is the summary of the mailbox:".
  - Use markdown formatting for headings and bullet points.
  - Format the summary as a list of key points and takeaways.
  
  # References
  - Use references to objects in the form of:
  @dxn:queue:data:B6INSIBY3CBEF4M5VZRYBCMAHQMPYK5AJ:01K24XMVHSZHS97SG1VTVQDM5Z:01K24XPK464FSCKVQJAB2H662M
  - References are rendered as rich content in the response to the user.
  
  # Tasks
  At the end of the summary include tasks.
  Extract only the tasks that are:
  - Directly actionable.
  - Clearly assigned to a person or team (or can easily be inferred).
  - Strongly implied by the conversation and/or user note (no speculative tasks).
  - Specific enough that someone reading them would know exactly what to do next.
  
  Format all tasks as markdown checkboxes using the syntax:
  - [ ] Task description.
  
  Additional information can be included (indented).
  
  # Email extraction
  Parse confirmation emails (e.g., flight bookings or hotel reservations) into structured objects.
  
  Note: Sending emails is handled by the "Inbox (Send)" blueprint.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.readEmail) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.
<input>{}</input>

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Inbox" [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You manage my email inbox.
  
  # Summary formatting:
  - Format the summary as a markdown document without extra comments like "Here is the summary of the mailbox:".
  - Use markdown formatting for headings and bullet points.
  - Format the summary as a list of key points and takeaways.
  
  # References
  - Use references to objects in the form of:
  @dxn:queue:data:B6INSIBY3CBEF4M5VZRYBCMAHQMPYK5AJ:01K24XMVHSZHS97SG1VTVQDM5Z:01K24XPK464FSCKVQJAB2H662M
  - References are rendered as rich content in the response to the user.
  
  # Tasks
  At the end of the summary include tasks.
  Extract only the tasks that are:
  - Directly actionable.
  - Clearly assigned to a person or team (or can easily be inferred).
  - Strongly implied by the conversation and/or user note (no speculative tasks).
  - Specific enough that someone reading them would know exactly what to do next.
  
  Format all tasks as markdown checkboxes using the syntax:
  - [ ] Task description.
  
  Additional information can be included (indented).
  
  # Email extraction
  Parse confirmation emails (e.g., flight bookings or hotel reservations) into structured objects.
  
  Note: Sending emails is handled by the "Inbox (Send)" blueprint.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.readEmail) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.
<input>{}</input>

1. Create a Mailbox object with typename `org.dxos.type.mailbox`
2. Enable the inbox blueprint
3. Call ReadEmail operation for that mailbox

Let me start by creating the mailbox and enabling the inbox blueprint in parallel.

  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.

    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.readEmail) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.
<input>{}</input>

The mailbox schema references the inbox blueprint in its annotations, but since it's not actually registered, I can't enable it or access the ReadEmail tool. I'll need to report this limitation accurately.

The mailbox object does exist in the database, but the inbox blueprint isn't available, so I'm unable to enable it or use the email reading functionality.

After refreshing and querying again, the inbox blueprint (`org.dxos.blueprint.inbox`) is still not in the registry. There is no blueprint with that key among the available ones. Without the inbox blueprint, the `read-email` tool is not available.

Let me report the results:

failure.description:
Step 1 succeeded: A Mailbox object was created successfully in the database (id: 01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTHF, typename: org.dxos.type.mailbox, name: 'Test Mailbox').

Step 2 failed: Attempted to enable blueprint with key 'org.dxos.blueprint.inbox' using enable-blueprints. The tool returned: {"enabled":[], "rejected":[{"key":"org.dxos.blueprint.inbox", "reason":"Blueprint not found in registry."}]}. After calling refresh-blueprints and query-blueprints again, the blueprint still does not exist. The available blueprint keys are: org.dxos.blueprint.agent, org.dxos.blueprint.agent-wizard, org.dxos.blueprint.assistant, org.dxos.blueprint.blueprint-manager, org.dxos.blueprint.browser, org.dxos.blueprint.database, org.dxos.blueprint.discord, org.dxos.blueprint.linear, org.dxos.blueprint.memory, org.dxos.blueprint.planning, dxos.org/blueprint/automation, org.dxos.blueprint.web-search. None of these is an inbox blueprint.

Step 3 failed: Without the inbox blueprint enabled, no read-email tool is available, so ReadEmail (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.readEmail) cannot be called.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]

  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.readEmail) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.readEmail) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.
<input>{}</input>

3. Call ReadEmail operation for that mailbox

Let me start by creating the mailbox and querying available blueprints simultaneously.
I'll start by creating the Mailbox object and querying available blueprints in parallel.

content.text:
Let me analyze the results:

1. The Mailbox creation failed because it requires a `feed` field (which is a reference to a feed object). I need to create the mailbox properly with the required fields.

2. The inbox blueprint (`org.dxos.blueprint.inbox`) was rejected with "Blueprint not found in registry." It's not in the available blueprints list either.

3. Looking at the mailbox schema, it requires: `feed` (a reference to a feed), `filters` (an array), and `id`.

Let me first create the mailbox properly. I need to create it with a feed reference. But feed is a declaration schema - let me check if I can create a feed object first, or if the system auto-creates it.


The feed field references a system type that I don't see in the schema list, so it might be auto-created. I'm going to try creating the mailbox with just the required fields—an empty filters array and a feed reference—to see if the system handles it automatically. I think the feed might be auto-created in ECHO systems when declared, so let me just try building the mailbox with the required fields and see if that works.

The inbox blueprint was rejected with "Blueprint not found in registry." I need to properly create the Mailbox (it requires a `feed` reference). Let me first try to create a feed object, then create the mailbox with it.
I now have a feed object with ID `01JGFJJZ00G0WKQSJGMAKCNTG1`. Now let me create the Mailbox with a reference to this feed. The mailbox requires `feed` and `filters` fields.
Good, the feed was created. Now let me create the Mailbox with the required fields.

The mailbox was created successfully. Now I need to:
1. ✅ Mailbox object exists in the database
2. The inbox blueprint (org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) was rejected with "Blueprint not found in registry." - I should report this exact error.
3. I need to try calling read-email. But I don't have a read-email tool available. Let me check what tools I have.

Looking at the available tools, I don't see a "read-email" tool. The inbox blueprint would have provided that tool, but it couldn't be enabled. Let me check if there's another way.


The instructions reference a ReadEmail operation that should come from the inbox blueprint, but since that blueprint isn't in the registry, I don't have access to this tool. I should try calling it anyway to see if it exists, or I may need to report that the blueprint dependency is blocking me from completing this step.
The inbox blueprint was not found. Let me try refreshing blueprints from the registry and then try again.

Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.

Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.

    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "echo:/YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "echo:/XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

You are an agent running in the non-interactive mode.
The user is unable to see what you are doing, and cannot answer any questions.
Do not ask questions.
Complete the task before you, and at the end call [completeJob] with the output.
If you are unable to complete the task, call [completeJob] with the failure reason.
If no output is required, call [completeJob] with an empty object: {}
Do not stop until you call [completeJob].

You are running within a test environment.
The prompt is the specification for the test.
Perform the instructions precisely and do not deviate.
Do not fake any work if the provided tools don't work.
The goal is to test the system, so be honest about the results.
If available tools prevented you from completing the task fully, report the failure.
Do not fall back on your own knowledge, only use the tools provided.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You can query available blueprints and enable them in the current conversation.
  Use [query-blueprints] to refresh the list of available blueprints.
  Use [refresh-blueprints] to update blueprint objects stored in the database from the built-in registry when they have drifted.
  Use [enable-blueprints] to enable blueprints by their keys. Always call [query-blueprints] first.
  Only blueprints with agentCanEnable=true can be enabled by the agent.
  
  <available_blueprints>
  -  "Agent blueprint"
  -  "Agent Wizard" -- Help the user create a new agent (subscriptions, optional cron timer, sync-triggers after edits). [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Assistant"
  -  "Blueprint Manager" -- Query and enable blueprints in the current conversation.
  -  "Browser" -- Access to a real isolated browser. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Database" -- Query and manipulate objects in the ECHO database. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Discord" -- Discord integration.
  -  "Linear" -- Syncs Linear workspaces.
  -  "Memory" -- Persistent memory storage and retrieval. [agent-can-enable]
  -  "Planning" -- Plans and tracks complex tasks using artifacts.
  -  "Triggers" -- Trigger management and automation.
  -  "Web Search" -- Search the web. [agent-can-enable]
  </available_blueprints>
  
  NOTE: You must enable the blueprint to use it, only then the tools from that blueprint will appear.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

The database starts empty.

First, create a Mailbox object in the space using the database blueprint tools (typename org.dxos.type.mailbox). Give it a clear name.

Then enable the inbox blueprint (key: org.dxos.blueprint.inbox) using the blueprint manager.

Call the ReadEmail operation (org.dxos.plugin.inbox.operation.readEmail) for that mailbox. With no messages in the feed, it should return zero emails — the important part is that ReadEmail completes successfully, not the count.

Completion criteria:
- A Mailbox object exists in the database.
- The inbox blueprint is successfully enabled, or you report the exact tool error if it cannot be enabled.
- You have called [read-email] and it completes successfully.
<input>{}</input>
